EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pacific storm brought significant rain and snow to northern Arizona. The most significant snow began to accumulate over northern Arizona on the afternoon of January 6th...and diminished from west to east across the area on January 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across northern Arizona on January 27 and 28th. The storm brought periods of moderate to heavy rain and higher elevation snow along with gusty winds. Snow levels started out between 7000 and 8000 feet then dropped to around 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds created traffic problems and road closures across the Western Mogollon Rim. Wind gusts to 37 MPH blew the 3 to 6 inches of fresh snow into drifts. Law Enforcement agencies in the area reported 33 traffic accidents and 50 slide-offs/motorist assists. Several area roads were closed due to blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of storm systems resulted in heavy rainfall in a few areas. Carefree Ranch reported a 24 hour total of 1.30 inches on Monday. The runoff from these recent rains left some low spots and highway dips flooded. Motorists who tried to cross low spots and washes in northern Maricopa County had to be rescued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency crews rescued a 61 year old man who attempted to drive his pickup truck across Cave Creek Wash at Desert Hills Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rainfall amounts were reported over two days after a plume of subtropical air moved into Arizona. The heaviest amounts occurred over the higher terrain generally east and north of Phoenix, resulting in flooded roads and other low-lying areas. About 3 inches fell at McDowell Mountain Park near Fountain Hills. The peak release from Granite Reef Dam into the Salt River was about 22,000 cfs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maricopa County Sheriffs office reports multiple road closures along the Cave Creek. Numerous washes were running in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported about 80 people had to be evacuated as flood waters from the Tonto Creek affected portions of Tonto Basin. A spotter in Tonto Basin reported part of a road being washed out. A power outage affected about 2,300 homes and was blamed on flooding when power poles near the creek were taken down. A small dike gave way which resulted in additional flooding of homes and roads. The Red Cross opened a shelter at the Tonto Basin Kiwanis Club and the Tonto Basin School. Gila County declared an emergency due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic cold mid to upper level trough originating near the Gulf of Alaska dug into Southeast Arizona providing Southeast Arizona with a cold rain and relatively low elevation snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous locations in Graham County below 5000 feet had in excess of 2 inches of snowfall. Higher elevations across Southeast Arizona above 7000 feet had 7 to 15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems moved through Southeast Arizona on December 25th and 26th. The system on December 25th was a lead system that moved through and produced rain and mountain snowfall generally above 6500 feet. The follow up system on December 26th was a much colder storm with origins in the Gulf of Alaska that moved through Southeast Arizona with much lower snow levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevations above 6000 feet in Graham county had in excess of 1 foot of snowfall.

A nearby eucalyptus tree was also struck, which resulted in pieces of wood or bark  exploding outward in all directions. Minor damage occurred to one side of a home near the school.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A railroad bridge over the Gilson Wash collapsed which resulted in a train derailment. Considerable damage resulted, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale trough of low pressure brought two back to back storms to northern Arizona over much of a four day period. These storms brought close to two feet of new snow over the Western Mogollon Rim at 7000 feet and close to four feet of new snow above 9000 feet. The first storm was cold enough to lower the snow level to the floor of the Grand Canyon. The snow level rose to the 7000 foot level for an hour or two with the second storm. Dring this event the Department of Public Safety for northern Arizona responded to 188 slideoffs on highways in the northern region. Officers also responded to 65 collisions, 12 of which involved injury. Two people were transported to Flagstaff Medical Center to be treated for their injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4.5 miles west of Ash Fork reported 12 inches of new snow by noon on December 16th. It was still snowing hard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized amounts of rainfall in excess of 8 inches occurred on Mt. Lemmon causing extensive flooding and rock slides. Several people in Tucson needed to be rescued from flowing washes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of winter storms brought significant snowfall to portions of Northern Arizona from the afternoon of December 22 through early morning of December 23. The heaviest snow from this system fell across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains with Flagstaff receiving 7 to 10 inches of new snow and the Arizona Snowbowl received 12 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to ten inches of new snow fell in Flagstaff from the afternoon of December 22 to the morning of the 23rd.  Eight to ten inches of fell in Williams and Parks. Six inches fell at Sunset Crater visitors center. Snow and blowing snow closed a 15 mile section (mile post 235 to 250) of Highway 180 near Kendrick Park north of Flagstaff on the way to the Grand Canyon.  The Flagstaff Police department responded to 49 vehicle collisions and 30 motorist assists.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling over the area during the afternoon of December 15th. By the morning of December 18th,  there was about two feet of new snow on the ground at the 7000 foot level. The snow caused many traffic accidents, power outages, and business/school closures and/or delayed  openings. The City of Flagstaff Police responded to 80 slideoffs, 61 non-injury collisions, and nine injury collisions during this event. Coconino County sheriff deputies responded to 35 slideoffs and 10 non-injury collisions.  Between Williams and Flagstaff, 10 to 13 inches of snow had fallen by the evening of December 16th.  A spotter at near 9000 feet had a storm total of 38 inches. Munds Park had 14 inches of snow by 8 AM on the 16th with snow still falling.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Western and Central Arizona Christmas night through the afternoon of December 26th. A variety of winter weather moved across the area including heavy snow, blowing snow, drifting snow, strong winds, and heavy rain in the lower elevations. Some warm air was pulled into the state ahead of the front...this caused snow levels to be higher than what was originally expected. Much of the heavier snow fell above 7500 feet...with a mix of rain...sleet...and snow from 6500-7000 feet. However...very strong winds also accompanied this front...causing severe blowing and drifting snow...as well as reduced visibility. Some roofs collapsed in the Flagstaff area from the weight of accumulated snow and ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought high winds to the Little Colorado River in Apache County.  The ASOS at St Johns Airport had a peak wind gust of 59 MPH. 5.5 inches of new snow fell in Concho.

A variety of winter weather moved across the area including heavy snow, blowing snow, drifting snow, strong winds, and heavy rain in the lower elevations. Some warm air was pulled into the state ahead of the front...this caused snow levels to be higher than what was originally expected. Much of the heavier snow fell above 7500 feet...with a mix of rain...sleet...and snow from 6500-7000 feet. However...very strong winds also accompanied this front...causing severe blowing and drifting snow...as well as reduced visibility. Some roofs collapsed in the Flagstaff area from the weight of accumulated snow and ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced high winds (40 MPH sustained with gusts to 60 MPH), blowing and drifting snow, and very low visibility.  Drifting snow blocked roads. The high wind caused short power outages and electrical sparks/flashes from power lines. Snow and rain from this storm combined with snow and rain already accumulated from other December storms caused the roofs to collapse on at least five businesses in Flagstaff. Many car port awnings in apartment complexes also collapsed under the weight of the snow and rain. Williams and Flagstaff received 6 inches of new snow. Parks reported 7 inches of snow and drifting snow.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Western and Central Arizona Christmas night through the afternoon of December 26th. A variety of winter weather moved across the area including heavy snow, blowing snow, drifting snow, strong winds, and heavy rain in the lower elevations. Some warm air was pulled into the state ahead of the front...this caused snow levels to be higher than what was originally expected. Much of the heavier snow fell above 7500 feet...with a mix of rain...sleet...and snow from 6500-7000 feet. However...very strong winds also accompanied this front...causing severe blowing and drifting snow...as well as reduced visibility. Some roofs collapsed in the Flagstaff area from the weight of accumulated snow and ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought high winds to the Little Colorado River in Coconino.  The ASOS at the Winslow Airport less than 5 miles across the Coconino County line had a peak wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains and melting snow have caused flooding and mud problems across the Navajo Nation. As many as 2,500 residents in the Tselani/Cottonwood area could only travel at night when the mud froze. A state of emergency was declared from January 29th to March 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and melting snow caused may of the roads across Navajo Nation to become impassible.  A state of emergency was declared from January 29 to March 31st. The Navajo Nation Council allocated $25,000 emergency funds for each of the Chapters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust measured at 46 mph at the airport in Yuma. Winds also damaged a roof of a bank building at 16th Street and 4th Avenue in downtown Yuma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across northern Arizona on January 27 and 28th. The storm brought periods of moderate to heavy rain and higher elevation snow along with gusty winds. Snow levels started out between 7000 and 8000 feet then dropped to around 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Silver Creek to flood. A woman driving on a street had to be rescued when her car got stuck in the rising flood water.||Show Low Creek ran up to the bottom of a bridge became a lake. Show Low Public Works closely surveyed the situation but, did not have to close the bridge. Show Low Lake filled and was running uncontrolled over the spillway 30 inches deep.||Water flowing over the earthen spillway of Bootleg Lake into Corduroy Creek prompted the evacuation of several homes downstream in the town of Carrizo.||The Three Pipes land bridge in the town of White River was breached and a foot bridge was washed away. Some families in the area were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe winds from thunderstorms that developed in New Mexico moved west into Cochise County causing strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow winds from a thunderstorm complex caused severe winds across the Douglas area. Strong winds blew down a storage shed in Douglas, knocked down several trees and caused a partial roof collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed in a very moist environment across Southeast Arizona resulting in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on the Mexican side of the border caused flash flooding in the city of Nogales, Arizona. This was caused by a damaged portion of the underground Nogales Wash. Local emergency management reported that water burst through the underground wash onto the surface just across the International Border. The border wall acted as a dam, keeping most of the flooding on the Mexican side. However, some water did flow through the port of entry into downtown Nogales, Arizona. Several businesses in the downtown business district experienced flooding and two illegal immigrants found two days later in the underground wash are also believed to have drowned due to this flooding. In addition, three illegal immigrants in an underground flood channel beneath the international border were rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed in a very moist environment across Southeast Arizona resulting in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported across Highway 191 with 2.5 inches of rainfall in one hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof blew off an adobe house in the village of Santa Rosa on the Tohono O'odham Nation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture resulted in scattered thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall across Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 75 mph occurred at Douglas-Bisbee airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense snow shower caused near zero visibility and slick roads that were blamed for a massive pileup on both sides of I-40.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense snow shower lead to a massive pileup in both directions on I-10 about 7 miles west of Flagstaff.  A total of 139 vehicles were involved over a 4 mile stretch on both sides of the highway. The east bound lanes were closed for over 14 hours and the west bound lanes were closed for over 16 hours. A total of 20 miles of I-40 were closed in both directions. This was one of the worst traffic accidents (most cars) in northern Arizona history. Two people died, 10 people had serious injuries, and another 35 people received treatment. A total of 53 people were transported to the Flagstaff Medical Center. Other patients were taken to Williams Health Care Center and Yavapai Regional Medical Center.  Fifteen people were extracted from their vehicles with specialized rescue equipment. About 40 people went to a Red Cross shelter that had been set up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong high pressure system over the region resulted in a prolonged period of very hot conditions. In Phoenix, a record high of 115 was set on the 21st...while in Yuma a record high of 116 was set on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong high pressure system resulted in record highs across the deserts. Phoenix tied the record on June 21 when the temperature hit 115 degrees. The average June temperature in Phoenix was 93.2 degrees, making it the fifth hottest June on record. In Maricopa County alone, 6 heat-related or heat-caused deaths were attributed to the heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pacific storm brought significant rain and snow to northern Arizona. The most significant snow began to accumulate over northern Arizona on the afternoon of January 6th...and diminished from west to east across the area on January 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff airport received 19 inches of snow from January 5 through January 7th. Hart Prairie (at 9200 feet) received 32 inches. Schools were closed in the Flagstaff area including Northern Arizona University. Law enforcement officers responded to over 180 slide offs and motorist assists, over 90 collisions, and over a dozen minor injuries in the Flagstaff area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles and trees were reported down at Chandler Heights and Greenfield roads, as well as Ocotillo and Higley and at Ocotillo and Power roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall totals across parts of South-Central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record daily maximum rainfall of 1.30 inches was set at Sky Harbor Airport. Other reports of heavy rain included .90 inch in 20 minutes in Tempe, and 1.00 inch in 20 minutes near Wickenburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly two inches of rain caused streets in Florence to become flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture as a result of Tropical Storm Julio produced thunderstorms with gusty winds and heavy rainfall across Western Cochise and Eastern Pima Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pantano Wash near Vail rose 9 feet in one hour and rose about 2 feet out of its banks per the USGS Stream Gage. This flash flooding moved downstream to the Harrison Road low water crossing where several feet of water crossed the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed over the city of Tucson resulting in a microburst causing wind damage to much of the north side of the city. In addition to the extensive damage, flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive wind damage occurred on the north side of Tucson due to a microburst. Damage reported included 28 power poles knocked down including a dozen at River Road and Dodge Blvd resulting in 35,000 customers without power, some for an extended period as much as two days. Damage was also reported at the Jewish Community Center near River Road and Alvernon Way. There was also roof damage to numerous businesses and hundreds of trees knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure along with a cold front brought very strong winds to northern Arizona on June 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 15 miles southwest of Winslow reported a peak wind gust of 62 MPH on his weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure along with a cold front brought very strong winds to northern Arizona on June 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds blew out numerous windows and caused extensive damage to Cliffdwellers Lodge near Vermillion Cliffs during the late afternoon on June 4th. The winds, possibly enhanced by the nearby cliffs, blew out windows in 5 parked cars and several of the motel rooms in the Lodge. The housing units also had broken windows, some damaged awnings, and and damaged trailer skirtings. Both the Lee's Ferry and Marble Canyon Lodges reported high winds but, no damage. Blowing dust made it difficult to even see across Highway 89A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the eastern slopes of the San Fransisco Peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell on the east side of the San Fransisco Peaks.  Sunset Crater reported 11 inches of snow and 7 to 8 inches of snow was reported in Doney Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person was stuck in a vehicle at a wash on Highway 19 south of Sells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture moved into Southeast Arizona as a result of a Gulf Surge from Tropical Storm Julio. Locally heavy rainfall and isolated severe thunderstorms occurred across Pima and Southwest Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a building at Rincon/University High Schools blowing apart several cinder blocks and strewing them across a patio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture produced severe thunderstorms across the City of Safford causing a power pole to be knocked down at 2nd St and Thatcher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was knocked down at 2nd Ave. and Thatcher St. in Safford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning in the vicinity of a low pressure system aloft. This produced areas of flash flooding especially in the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swift water rescue occurred at the CDO Wash and Overton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of severe thunderstorms moved westward across the central and eastern portions of Maricopa County. Upper level winds were stronger than usual, and copious moisture combined with warm temperatures allowed storms to redevelop well into the night. Winds over 80 mph were noted in parts of Phoenix and Tempe. Nearly continuous lightning was also observed during the peak of the activity. Fortunately, no fatalities were associated with these severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread downpours were reported around the Valley with generally one to two inches of rain in a short period. One of the heaviest amounts was 1.89 inches at Gateway Community College in Phoenix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of severe thunderstorms moved westward across the central and eastern portions of Maricopa County. Upper level winds were stronger than usual, and copious moisture combined with warm temperatures allowed storms to redevelop well into the night. Winds over 80 mph were noted in parts of Phoenix and Tempe. Nearly continuous lightning was also observed during the peak of the activity. Fortunately, no fatalities were associated with these severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a wind gust of 85 mph at 16th St and Thomas. Widespread damage occurred to homes, businesses and windows knocked out in at least one high-rise. Numerous power poles were taken down, and many trees uprooted. Some damage also occurred at the Arizona State Capitol in Phoenix.

Winds over 80 mph were noted in parts of Phoenix and Tempe. Nearly continuous lightning was also observed during the peak of the activity. Fortunately, no fatalities were associated with these severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Tempe, an 18 year-old man was injured by a falling tree. Winds on the ASU campus were measured at 69 mph and severely damaged the indoor football practice facility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed between 5 and 6 am, with rainfall rates of one to 2 inches in a small area of southern Gila County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved slowly across southern Gila County during the early morning hours. Considerable runoff led to water in some small streams and creeks. By 730 am, water was reported deep in Pinal Creek with water and small debris flowing rapidly across highway 60 in downtown Globe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms and very heavy rain resulted in flooded low spots and highway dips near Bouse. Centennial wash near Wenden was flooded and gauges recorded a flow of 6,000 cubic feet per second with a stage near 4 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle with 5 women was swept away as it was caught in raging flood waters along route 72, about a mile and a half southeast of Bouse. One woman died and her body was found about 7 miles away. An RV was also stranded by flood waters in the same wash at the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds took down power poles in Yuma and San Luis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty winds, along with heavy rain rolled through parts of  Yuma and San Luis. Arizona Public Service reported 26 power poles were knocked down in San Luis with seven main lines impacted. Trees were uprooted and caused property damage. At the Yuma airport, winds were clocked at 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail and strong winds accompanied some of the storms that moved slowly through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Globe Sheriffs Office reported 3/4 inch diameter hail. Trained spotters in Globe estimated wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and locally severe thunderstorms slammed into the Punkin Center and Roosevelt areas on Friday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail about the size of pennies was reported at Roosevelt Estates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of southwest and south-central Arizona. A few storms became severe, with strong winds, hail and very heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 6 power poles were downed, resulting in power outages for as many as 4500 customers in Queen Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall totals across parts of South-Central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets and highways reported flooded in Phoenix and Tempe. One spotter near Guadalupe and McClintock picked up 2 1/2 inches in just 45 minutes. One unofficial report near Baseline and I-10 included 3.75 inches from this storm. A roof of a charter school in Ahwatukee was reported to have collapsed from the accumulated rain water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm hit Strawberry and produced nickle sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced nickle sized hail that accumulated on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous power poles were blown down and homes were damaged in East Mesa when severe thunderstorms hit the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds took down a total of 55 power poles in Mesa, leaving as many as 12,000 SRP customers without power. About 31 homes were damaged at a trailer park on North Recker, 4 of those had roofs blown off. On Southern Ave near Power Road, 15 poles were knocked down with lines impacting 7 vehicles, including a bus. The peak wind speed at Falcon Field was 44 mph at 7:47 pm. In Mesa, power poles were knocked down, trapping 6 vehicles, including a bus. One of the injuries was from cuts from broken glass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern and central portions of Arizona were very moist and unstable. Storms developed and moved toward the southwest and strong winds kicked up widespread areas of blowing dust. A Severe Thunderstorm Watch was in effect for much of the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds reported at Brown and Mesa. Trees were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern and central portions of Arizona were very moist and unstable. Storms developed and moved toward the southwest and strong winds kicked up widespread areas of blowing dust. A Severe Thunderstorm Watch was in effect for much of the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several crashes on the Loop 202 were blamed on strong winds and rain. Power outages were reported after winds and rain moved through the East Valley. SRP reported about 3,000 customers were left without electricity...and APS reported 2,000 customers without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed near Coolidge and caused strong winds and heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five power poles blown down at Randolph and Eleven Mile Corner roads. A police car was damaged by a dumpster that was blown around by the wind. Trees were uprooted in Coolidge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and locally severe thunderstorms slammed into the Punkin Center and Roosevelt areas on Friday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds knocked a 40 foot motor home on its side and took down power poles and trees in Punkin Center. Winds damaged a 20 foot section of a roof and blew down a TV antenna at Roosevelt Lakes Estates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms developed across portions of Cochise County producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred at Coronado National Memorial causing flash flooding as washes overflowing the roadway causing significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very isolated and small thunderstorm occurred near the Safford airport with strong winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Safford airport ASOS reported a wind gust to 56 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat that started in June continued into early July. Strong high pressure in the upper atmosphere helped to produce record warm minimum temperatures in Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat was blamed as a factor in 9 deaths in the month of July for Maricopa County. Temperatures in Phoenix were above normal for much of the month, with this month being tied for the 17th warmest on record. The average minimum temperature was the 10th warmest, and a record |warm minimum of 90 degrees was set on July 1st. The high temperature in Phoenix on the 1st was 110.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning in the vicinity of a low pressure system aloft. This produced areas of flash flooding especially in the Tucson metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Motorist stranded in flooded road near Beverly Road and 29th Street in Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed in a very moist environment across Southeast Arizona resulting in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rainfall occurred in the border town of Lukeville, Arizona. This caused flooding and damage to private property, businesses and government offices with water several feet high. A report from officials at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument stated a recently built fence along the international border exacerbated the flooding as a result of the fence acting like a dam and stopping the normal progression of water through the border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with wet microbursts developed in a very moist environment across portions of Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported three quarters of an inch sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed across Southeast Arizona resulting in heavy rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several swift water rescues occurred in the Tucson metro area including along Wentworth Road in Corona De Tucson and in Northeast Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Southeast Arizona resulting in large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pantano Wash at Vail crested at 12.6 feet or 1.6 feet above bankful at 9:00 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Southeast Arizona resulting in large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 0.90 inch of rain in 15 minutes with roadways flooded in the Sahuarita area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms occurred across much of Southeast Arizona resulting in areas of flash flooding and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was flooded in the roadway near Grant Road and Alvernon Road in Tucson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Diamond Creek Road north of Peach Springs was closed due to high water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Pierce Ferry Road and Stockton Hill Road was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced very heavy rain in Fort Valley Estates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Fort Valley Estates (on the northwest edge of Flagstaff) reported 3 inches of rain in 20 minutes. Rio de Flag and other streams were starting to flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail and strong winds accompanied some of the storms that moved slowly through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 1.20 inches of rain fell in 18 minutes, resulting in street flooding. A trained spotter in Globe also had very heavy rain, with 1.36 inches in only 25 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low visiblity due to blowing dust resulted from strong winds from nearby thunderstorms. Winds were generally in the 30 to 40 mph range with reports of blowing dust in the Phoenix East Valley late in the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coolidge police reported dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through parts of the East Valley, and cloud to ground lightning started a fire in the attic of a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning was blamed in starting a fire in the attic of a Tempe home. Tempe Fire responded to two other weather related fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 3/4 inch hail near Show Low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter 8 miles southwest of Show Low reported 3/4 inch diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient moisture and instability together with an outflow boundary from the east was sufficient to trigger thunderstorms in Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scottsdale airport recorded peak winds of 53 mph during thunderstorms. Winds at Sky Harbor airport reached as high as 39 mph and some tents at the Tempe Town Lake fireworks display were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second thunderstorm produces periods of heavy rain and hail in Ash Fork.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized (7/8 inch diameter) hail fell for the second time in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second thunderstorm produces periods of heavy rain and hail in Ash Fork.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported by the Yavapai County Sheriffs' Office in the Juniper Wood subdivision of Ash Fork. Radar estimated 2 to 4 inches of rain had fallen in that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms over a period of 3 days lead to flash flooding in Cataract Canyon/Havasu Canyon.  The flooding lasted several days and caused damage to a few homes in the Village of Supai. There was extensive damage to Havasu Canyon and the campground below the village. Many campers were stranded during the night in trees and on picnic tables.  In all, 406 people were flown out of Havasu Canyon by helicopters from multiple public safety agencies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms over the Cataract Creek and Havasu Creek drainages caused flash flooding that started in the Village of Supai around 11:30 PM on August 16. Radar estimated between 3 and 4 inches of rain over several square miles in the Cataract Creek drainage during the afternoon.  Additional heavy rain from thunderstorms on August 17 continued heavy run off into the canyon. Cataract Canyon/Creek becomes Havasu Canyon/Creek at Havasu Springs about a mile upstream from the Village of Supai. This very popular destination for tourists was closed for at least 6 months because of the heavy damage caused by the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a tornado and hail northwest of Valle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado was reported abut 15 miles northwest of Valle. Several funnel clouds were seen intermittently for 20 minutes after last touchdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced accumulating hail, heavy rain, and wind gusts to 30 MPH in and around the community of Parks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized (7/8 inch diameter) hail fell in and around Parks. The hail covered the ground like snow. A spotter reported 4 inches of hail on the ground. The wind was gusting to 30 MPH during this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures in Phoenix for the first three days of August were the warmest on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very warm temperatures were reported throughout Maricopa County. Low temperatures in Phoenix for the first three days of August were records: 95, 91 and 89 degrees. There were 2 heat-related deaths reported in the county during this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms caused power outages in south Gilbert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds knocked a power line onto the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved slowly across the higher terrain and dumped heavy rain, small hail and brief strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated gusts to about 58 mph. Another spotter in Globe had a measured gust of 45 mph at 4:55 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 51 mph at Sky Harbor airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines down near 7th Street and Northern.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds gusted up to 65 knots at Sky Harbor airport. Several aircraft and at least one terminal building was damaged, with debris blown onto the runways and adjacent areas. About 500 people were stranded in the terminals overnight due to flight delays and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter sized hail in Cornville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced nickel to quarter sized (3/4 to 1 inch diameter) hail in Cornville. Heavy rain also caused some minor flooding problems on local roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced one inch diameter hail near Cross Mountain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced one inch diameter hail southwest of Seligman near Cross Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with severe thunderstorms affected parts of Yuma late Thursday night and early Friday morning. These storms were part of a huge system that moved through the Phoenix area earlier that night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted and a semi trailer was turned over. A peak gust of 57 mph was measured at the Yuma airport. About 1,000 APS customers were left without power due to these thunderstorm winds. Power poles were blown down in the Mohawk area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from nearby thunderstorms caused significant damage to homes in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 20 trees were downed and several homes sustained roof damage due to the strong winds. At Deer Valley airport winds were measured at 51 mph at 10:55 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds over 70 mph damaged planes at Chandler Municipal Airport. Strong winds also blew down trees and damaged some homes in the Chandler area. Dense blowing dust was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds hit Chandler airport and flipped at least two planes. Winds also damaged a fence and other property. Northeast winds peaked at 67 mph at 3:25 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produces damaging winds,  heavy rain,  and pea sized hail in Star Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gila County Sheriff office reported a thunderstorm that knocked down trees with strong winds.  This storm also produce heavy rain and pea sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy showers and thunderstorms produced areas of Flash Flooding on the Southeast side of the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sahuarita Road at Houghton Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall in northern Pinal County resulted in flooded roads and low spots.  Radar estimated rainfall rates of one to two inches per hour in that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, with rainfall rates of one to 2 inches per hour caused washes to flow. A car was washed away attempting to cross a flooded road near Geronimo and 28th Avenue. A woman and a two year-old were rescued after the car became lodged under a small bridge. Another car was washed away near Val Vista and 16th Avenue. A nearby gage at Delaware and Apache Trail recorded about one inch in 30 minutes up to 641 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm brought damaging winds and heavy rain to Bridgeport in the Verde Vally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down. The trees were about a foot in diameter at the base. Fifteen power poles were blown down with about 4000 feet of line. The power was out for about 36 hours in portions of Bridgeport. An above ground pool was smashed to bits and crumpled like an accordion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced lightning that stuck a man that was working on a fence about 5 miles west of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was working on a fence when lightning hit nearby or hit the fence. The jolt knocked the man away and ripped the glove off his hand. He had numbness and tingling in his arm. He was transported to the hospital for observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced nickel sized hail near Payson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced dime to nickel sized (3/4 to 7/8 inch diameter) hail about one mile east-northeast of Payson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain produced a record rainfall at the Yuma airport on Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused some damage to the Hospice Thrift Store in Yuma.  Several businesses at Southgate Mall reported damage due to standing water. A new daily rainfall record,  0.74 inches,  was set at the Yuma Marine Corps Air Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds near 60 mph were associated with some thunderstorms in the Phoenix area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Top wind speeds of 55 to 65 mph were common across many areas. One report was a measured speed of 60 mph at 91st avenue and Jomax. A 58 mph gust was measured at the White Tank mesonet location. A wood fence was damaged and a trailer was overturned in Waddell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall totals across parts of South-Central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a microburst blew down about 25 trees and damaged light poles at Mesa Community College. A security officer was slightly injured when the strong winds blew him from his golf cart.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds caused power outages and property damage due to microburst winds as high as 65 mph. Winds blew down a tree near 78th Street and McDonald which damaged a covered parking structure.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Western and Central Arizona Christmas night through the afternoon of December 26th. A variety of winter weather moved across the area including heavy snow, blowing snow, drifting snow, strong winds, and heavy rain in the lower elevations. Some warm air was pulled into the state ahead of the front...this caused snow levels to be higher than what was originally expected. Much of the heavier snow fell above 7500 feet...with a mix of rain...sleet...and snow from 6500-7000 feet. However...very strong winds also accompanied this front...causing severe blowing and drifting snow...as well as reduced visibility. Some roofs collapsed in the Flagstaff area from the weight of accumulated snow and ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought high winds to the Little Colorado River in Navajo County.  The ASOS at the Winslow Airport had a peak wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful cold front moving across northern Arizona brought high winds,  power outages, and some damage to structures and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds broke branches of various sizes out of cottonwood trees at the mouth of Canyon de Chelly.  The winds also caused the power to go out in Chinle. The weather station at Red Mountain School had a peak gust of 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful cold front moving across northern Arizona brought high winds,  power outages, and some damage to structures and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blow the roofs off two single story single row apartment buildings.  One roof landed on a car causing further damage. An 18-wheeled tractor-trailer rig and a travel trailer blew over on I-40  about 6 miles east of Winslow. A large metal storage building was removed from the foundation due to high winds.  Several other 18 wheel trucks overturned on I-40 at mile markers 274 and 277 (about 20 miles east of Winslow).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over two inches of rain resulted in local flooding in Yuma. In fact, 2.22 inches was recorded at the airport, setting a new daily record. This was also the ninth wettest day ever, based upon records dating back to 1876.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain left streets and low spots flooded. Homes in the Yuma foothills and in south Yuma were flooded. One soccer field was soaked with 2 feet of standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of winter storms brought significant snowfall to portions of Northern Arizona from the afternoon of December 22 through early morning of December 23. The heaviest snow from this system fell across the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains with Flagstaff receiving 7 to 10 inches of new snow and the Arizona Snowbowl received 12 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of snow fell in Christopher Creek and Kohls Ranch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell with this storm in Show Low and Forest Lakes. The snow was also drifting in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved steadily toward the northeast during the afternoon hours. As a result, locally heavy rain, strong winds, and very low visibility due to dust and sand moved across the deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread areas of dense blowing dust spread across the metropolitan area. Winds of 40 to 50 mph were common. An automatic weather station at Tempe Town Lake recorded a peak wind gust of 51 mph. Flights were delayed at Sky Harbor Airport where the peak wind gust of 51 mph was recorded at 6:02 pm. Luke AFB had winds up to 48 mph that dramatically cut the visibility to a mere 1/8th mile just before 6 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased and the airmass warmed quickly by mid day. Thunderstorms formed first over the higher terrain and became severe as they moved toward the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quartzsite Fire Department reported an estimated gust of 75 mph that blew two tractor trailer trucks off the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture resulted in thunderstorm development across the Tucson Metro area with reports of wind damage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines knocked down at Wentworth and Wetstones Road near New Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of southwest and south-central Arizona. A few storms became severe, with strong winds, hail and very heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Somerton police reported power lines down due to very strong winds from thunderstorms. At the peak of the storm, between 2,500 and 3,000 APS customers were without power. A large tree in the 3300 block of 15th Avenue in The Dunes subdivision was knocked down. Power outages were also reported on the Cocopah Reservation at County 18th Street and Avenue D and in the north end of the city of Yuma.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in Ehrenberg resulted in flooded low spots. Roads were closed between Parker and Poston.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to Arizona Public Service, 48 power poles across a distance of 3 miles were blown down along Old Highway 80 between Buckeye and Gila Bend. Winds were measured up to 56 mph on the Palo Verde Nuclear Generating Station tower.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was reported across parts of central and western Maricopa County. Most of the reports were for dime to penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season push of monsoon moisture brought thunderstorms to northwest Arizona.  One storm produced severe hail.
Scattered thunderstorms developed across Pinal and Pima Counties resulting in strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage occurred at a hangar in Pinal airpark. In addition, several trees were uprooted and a large 747 airplane was moved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen off Highway 61 near Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rolled across portions of Graham and Eastern Pima Counties producing wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 3 inch diameter trees were blown down in Oro Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of the Tucson metro area producing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof was partially blown off a home near 22nd and Camino Seco in Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dust devil caused minor damage to homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor damage to homes resulted when a dust devil moved through this community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of severe thunderstorms moved westward across the central and eastern portions of Maricopa County. Upper level winds were stronger than usual, and copious moisture combined with warm temperatures allowed storms to redevelop well into the night. Winds over 80 mph were noted in parts of Phoenix and Tempe. Nearly continuous lightning was also observed during the peak of the activity. Fortunately, no fatalities were associated with these severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees blown down.
